FactCorrectionRequest.description:
The bi-temporal correction body for a fact edge (api-design §3 Item ops).

Carries the flat scope (``_ScopedBody``: at least one of user/session, a missing
pair is the boundary 422) plus the new ``object`` the fact should now assert. The
subject/relation are NOT settable — a correction keeps the same predicate and only
revises its value (a different subject/relation is a new fact, not a correction).
``confidence`` and ``t_valid`` are optional, defaulting in ``ops.update_fact`` (the
latter to now), and both are validated there before any DB round-trip or paid embed.

confidence.description: Confidence of the corrected edge, a [0,1] probability.
confidence.title: Confidence

object.description: The corrected object the fact should now assert.
object.title: Object

t_valid.description: When the correction becomes valid (aware UTC); defaults to now.
t_valid.title: T Valid

FactCorrectionRequest.title: FactCorrectionRequest

FactItem.description: The corrected fact edge in the flat wire shape (its new ``ft_`` id + text).

t_valid.description: When the corrected edge became valid.
t_valid.title: T Valid

text.description: The regenerated 'subject relation object' fact text.
text.title: Text

FactItem.title: FactItem

FilterClause.description:
One request-side read predicate, mapping straight onto the domain ``Filter``.

The wire shape of ``store.repos.filters`` input: ``field`` is an intrinsic column
(``importance``, ``event_time``, …) or ``metadata.<key>``; ``op`` is the
``FilterOp`` enum; ``value`` is the bound comparand. Validation of the field name
and operator against the per-arm whitelist happens in the compiler (which raises
``ValidationError`` → 422), NOT here — duplicating the whitelist on the wire model
would create a second source of truth that could drift from the SQL-safe one.

value.description: The comparand bound as a SQL parameter.
value.title: Value

FilterClause.title: FilterClause

ForesightItem.description: A surfaced forward-looking note (plan / temporary state / deadline).

id.description: Prefixed public id of the source episode (ep_<uuid>).
id.title: Id

id.title: Id

next_cursor.description: Opaque cursor for the next page; null when there are no more.
next_cursor.title: Next Cursor

changed.description: False when the episode was already in this state (or unknown).
changed.title: Changed

pinned.description: The protected state after the call (True=pinned).
pinned.title: Pinned

ProblemDetail.description:
An RFC-9457 ``application/problem+json`` body — the one error shape (api-design §5).

Every non-2xx response serializes to this so an SDK has a single error model to
generate against. ``type``/``title``/``status`` are the RFC core members;
``detail`` is the human-readable, SANITIZED explanation (never an internal stack
or a raw provider message for a 5xx). ``instance`` carries the request's
correlation id so a caller can quote it when reporting a failure. The model is
declared as a ``response_model`` on every route's ``responses={…}`` map so it
shows up in the committed OpenAPI artifact.

detail.description: A human-readable, sanitized explanation of this occurrence.
detail.title: Detail

instance.description: The request correlation id (X-Request-Id) for this occurrence.
instance.title: Instance

status.description: The HTTP status code for this occurrence.
status.title: Status

title.description: A short, human-readable summary of the problem type.
title.title: Title

type.description: A URI identifying the problem type; 'about:blank' for generic errors.
type.title: Type

ProblemDetail.title: ProblemDetail

last_outcome.description: The most recent recorded outcome, or null if never applied.
last_outcome.title: Last Outcome

Provenance.description:
The read-path provenance: how the context was routed, verified, and repaired (§3).

All three are null on the pure P0 path (no hot client wired): ``route`` is the
difficulty the router assigned, ``verifier`` whether the hard-path verifier judged
the context sufficient (the PRE-repair verdict that triggered any repair), and
``repaired`` whether one targeted repair round ran.

verifier.description: The hard-path verifier's pre-repair sufficiency verdict.
verifier.title: Verifier

Provenance.title: Provenance

RememberResourceRequest.description:
A blob handle (+ caption or captionable URL) to store (api-design §3).

The caller owns the blob store; ``blob_ref`` is the opaque handle Tessera persists.
Supply ``caption`` to store it verbatim, or ``image_url`` to have the VLM caption a
fetchable image. Exactly which is required is enforced downstream by
``resource.remember_resource`` (a missing-both case asks the VLM with no image and
surfaces as a ``ValueError`` → 422).

_ScopedBody.description:
The flat scope every write/wipe body carries: at least one of user/session (§2).

``user_id`` and ``session_id`` are client partition labels (NOT authenticated — the
tenant is); ``scoping.resolve_owner`` rejects the empty pair with the boundary 422.
Declared here so the writes share one definition of the scope fields rather than
redeclaring them per body.
_ScopedBody.title: _ScopedBody

tessera__api__v1__memories__JobHandle.title: JobHandle

get.description:
Readiness: 200 when the DB answers a guarded ``SELECT 1``, else 503.

Delegates to ``Database.healthcheck()`` (bounded by a fixed short timeout so a
wedged pool fails the probe fast rather than hanging the orchestrator). Returns an
explicit ``ORJSONResponse`` so the 503 carries the same ``{"status": …}`` body as
the 200 — a balancer reads the status code, but a human curling it sees why.

post.description:
Pack a user's panel summaries into one budgeted digest (one read, no LLM) (§3).

``ops.compress`` walks the user's panels newest-first and packs their summaries under
the requested ``token_budget`` (a ~4-chars-per-token heuristic, no tokenizer call),
returned ``200`` as a single ``digest`` block. The cheap "whole-user memory at a
glance" surface — no retrieval, no model reasoning.

Consolidate a user's Tray: enqueue a job (202) or run inline (200) (api-design §3).

By default this ENQUEUES a ``consolidate`` job on ``setter_jobs`` (a system table, no
RLS) tenant-scoped to the principal, returning ``202`` with the job handle to poll —
the LLM-heavy flush runs on the background worker. ``enqueue_job`` dedupes against an
already-active job for the same (tenant, user, kind), so a repeated request returns
that in-flight job's handle rather than piling on. ``?sync=true`` instead runs
``ops.consolidate`` INLINE under the bounded semaphore + timeout and returns ``200``
with the report; inline requires a hot client (a router-disabled deploy has no
consolidation engine, so it is a 422 rather than a 500), mirroring the sync write.

parameters.description: Run consolidation INLINE (200) instead of enqueuing (202).
parameters.name: sync

schema.description: Run consolidation INLINE (200) instead of enqueuing (202).
schema.title: Sync

get.description:
Resolve a ``job_<int>`` to its coarse status, tenant-filtered (api-design §3).

``decode_job`` rejects any non-``job_`` id with the boundary 422. The lookup runs on
``db.system()`` (``setter_jobs`` has no RLS) and filters by the principal's
``tenant_id`` IN SQL — the explicit tenant scope this endpoint depends on for
isolation. A row absent in the caller's tenant is a 404, leaking nothing about whether
that id belongs to another tenant.

delete.description:
Erase EVERYTHING for one user across every table (right-to-be-forgotten, §3).

``governance.wipe_user`` resolved to the body's owner (``resolve_owner`` — a missing
pair is 422). The riskiest verb in the surface, so scope is in the BODY (not a query
string a proxy might log) and it is tenant-scoped by the principal like every other
route. Returns the total rows erased; a single audit row survives the wipe by design.

get.description:
Keyset-paginated browse over a user's episodic memories, newest first (api-design §9).

Scope is the flat ``user_id``/``session_id`` query pair (``resolve_owner`` rejects an
empty pair with 422). Fetches ``limit + 1`` rows to detect a further page WITHOUT a
second count query: if the extra row is present it is dropped and its predecessor's
``(created_at, id)`` becomes the next cursor. A malformed cursor or a non-positive
limit is the boundary 422 (``decode_cursor``/``resolve_limit``).

parameters.description: Opaque keyset cursor from a prior page.
parameters.name: cursor

schema.description: Opaque keyset cursor from a prior page.
schema.title: Cursor

post.description:
Append one turn; async (201, queue) by default, sync (200, consolidate inline).

The hot write is identical in both modes — ``Memory.add`` embeds and durably stores
the turn, scoped by ``resolve_owner`` (a missing user/session pair is the boundary
422). ``mode=async`` returns immediately with the (possibly null) consolidation job
handle; ``mode=sync`` additionally awaits ``ops.consolidate`` under the bounded
semaphore + timeout so the caller gets the freshly-derived facts/episodes back in
one round trip (ADR 0004). ``infer=false`` is a RAW store (api-design §3): it is
threaded to ``Memory.add`` so the async consolidation cadence is never enqueued, and
it short-circuits the sync branch BEFORE the inline ``ops.consolidate`` — a raw write
asks for no derive in either mode, so ``infer=false`` always returns the async-add
body with no job, even under ``mode=sync``.

delete.description:
Forget a raw Tray turn by its client id (api-design §3 Item ops).

The turn is addressed by its client/ULID ``turn_id`` (not a prefixed public id) since
that is what the caller wrote it with. ``governance.forget(turn_id=…)`` is a true
delete with no cascade (a raw turn links to no tessera); a turn that does not exist
yields a zero-count report (the erase is idempotent), not a 404.

Forget one memory by its prefixed id, dispatching on the prefix (api-design §3).

``ep_`` runs the full ``governance.forget`` tessera cascade (the episode, its
foresight, single-source facts, graph edges, panel/portrait reconciliation) — passed
``runtime.hot`` so a surviving panel is re-narrated by the LLM when one is wired, and
degrades to a deterministic placeholder when it is not. ``ft_`` deletes a single fact
edge. A ``job_`` id is the boundary 422. The erase is idempotent (a missing id yields
a zero-count report, not a 404), matching the governance contract.

get.description:
Fetch one memory by its prefixed id, dispatching on the prefix (api-design §2).

``ep_`` resolves a tessera, ``ft_`` a fact; a ``job_`` (or any other) id is the
boundary 422 (``decode``). The id alone does not encode the user, so the flat scope
is still required and enforced per-user in the repo (RLS isolates only the tenant). A
well-formed id that resolves to no row in the caller's (tenant, user) is a 404.

patch.description:
Correct a fact edge bi-temporally; ``ft_`` only, an ``ep_`` is 422 (api-design §3).

``decode_uuid(expected=FACT)`` accepts only an ``ft_`` id — an ``ep_`` (or any
other) prefix is the boundary 422 (an episode is not a single correctable
assertion). The new ``object``, confidence, and validity instant lower straight into
``ops.update_fact``, which supersedes the predecessor in one transaction and returns
the successor edge; a missing fact is its 404 and an already-invalidated /
concurrently-closed one its 409. The response carries the SUCCESSOR's ``ft_`` id so a
caller can address the corrected version, while an as-of-past read still finds the
superseded predecessor.

delete.description:
Clear an episode's pin, returning it to the sweep's reach; ``ep_`` only (§3).

The mirror of ``pin_memory``: ``decode_uuid(expected=EPISODE)`` is the same
``ep_``-only guard, and ``ops.unpin`` clears ``protected`` and returns whether the
flag changed (False when already unpinned or unknown). The result reports
``pinned=false`` with that ``changed`` flag.

post.description:
Pin an episode so the eviction sweep never demotes/evicts it; ``ep_`` only (§3).

``decode_uuid(expected=EPISODE)`` rejects an ``ft_`` (or any non-episode) id with the
boundary 422 — a fact is not pinnable. ``ops.pin`` sets ``protected = true`` and
returns whether the flag changed (False when already pinned or the id is unknown,
both audited); the response echoes the resulting ``pinned`` state and that
``changed`` flag rather than 404-ing an idempotent no-op, matching the op's contract.

Append a batch of turns sequentially under one scope (mem0-style, api-design §3).

Each message is one ``Memory.add`` in request order, sharing the body's scope and
metadata; a per-message ``turn_id`` (else a server-minted ULID) keeps each write
individually retry-safe. Sequential, not concurrent, because the consolidation
cadence counts turns and a batch is a single conversational burst — ordering keeps
the edge-trigger deterministic. Returns one async-add result per message so a caller
can see which turns were created and which (if any) queued a consolidation.

Store a how-to skill recallable by trigger similarity (api-design §3).

``procedural.remember_procedure`` embeds the trigger (the recall key) and inserts a
``protected`` procedural tessera; the stored skill is returned with fresh
reinforcement counters (``uses=0``, ``last_outcome=null``) and its ``ep_`` id so a
caller can later record an outcome against it.

Recall the learned skills whose trigger best matches a task (api-design §3).

``procedural.recall_procedures`` embeds the task and runs a KNN scoped to procedural
tesserae, returning each hit's parsed procedure and its cosine similarity, best-first;
``min_similarity`` drops weak matches so a task with no learned skill returns nothing
rather than a spurious nearest neighbor. A read — it does not touch use counts.

Record that a procedure was applied, updating its reinforcement state (api-design §3).

A procedure is addressed by its tessera's ``ep_`` id, so ``decode_uuid(expected=
EPISODE)`` rejects any non-episode id with the boundary 422.
``procedural.record_outcome`` atomically bumps ``uses`` and sets ``last_outcome``
(a failure still counts as a use — outcome is a usage signal, not a quality gate) and
raises ``NotFoundError`` → 404 when the id is unknown or not a procedural tessera. The
updated procedure (with the new counters) is returned.

Compose a memory context for a question (api-design §3 Reads, §1).

Lowers to ``Memory.query`` (which resolves the owner, rejects a blank query, runs the
hybrid arms + theme panels + tray, and — in chat mode with a hot client — the
difficulty router, verifier, and one repair round). The composed ``context`` is the
primary product; the structured pieces and the provenance are echoed so a caller can
cite, inspect, or audit how the context was assembled. ``filters`` and the bi-temporal
vantages thread straight through. An empty scope or a blank query is the boundary 422.

Repair derived index state synchronously — embeddings + panel drift (api-design §3).

``ops.reindex`` is deterministic (re-embeds null-embedding raw turns and recomputes
level-0 panel centroids from current membership) with no LLM and no background job
kind, so it runs INLINE and returns ``200`` with the repair counts directly. Scoped
to the body's owner.

Store a blob handle + caption as a retrievable resource (api-design §3, JSON).

The caller owns the blob storage; Tessera persists only the ``blob_ref`` handle and
the searchable caption (supplied, or VLM-generated from ``image_url``).
``resource.remember_resource`` embeds the caption on the episode tier, inserts a
``protected`` resource tessera, and audits it; the stored resource is returned with
its ``ep_`` id.

Thin multipart upload: VLM-caption + embed an image, then DISCARD the bytes (§3).

A convenience over the JSON route for callers that have the bytes in hand: the upload
is read into memory, validated against the image-MIME allowlist (422 otherwise) and a
5 MB size cap (413 otherwise) BEFORE the paid VLM call, base64-encoded, and handed to
``resource.remember_resource`` which captions it through the runtime's ``vlm`` and
embeds the caption (or the image itself, when the embedder is multimodal). The bytes
are NOT retained — only the caption and the caller-supplied ``blob_ref`` handle are
persisted (blob storage is out of scope). Scope is the flat user/session FORM fields.

Recall resources whose caption/visual content matches a text query (api-design §3).

``resource.recall_resources`` embeds the query and runs a KNN scoped to resource
tesserae (the first-class typed read path, reachable regardless of any episodic
``type`` filter), returning each resource and its cosine similarity best-first.

Structured RRF retrieval to flat typed results, no LLM (api-design §3 Reads).

Lowers straight to ``ops.retrieve`` (which resolves the owner, rejects a blank query,
and fuses dense + BM25 per arm), then flattens episodes and facts into one
``results[]`` ordered by descending score. ``rerank=false`` passes the in-process RRF
identity reranker so the raw fusion order stands without a hosted rerank call;
``rerank=true`` (the default) uses the runtime's configured reranker. ``types`` is
applied AFTER retrieval as a thin projection (the episode/fact arms are separate
queries, so restricting to one type simply drops the other arm's hits). A bad filter
field/op or an empty scope surfaces as the boundary 422.
